reasoning: The question asks whether the Federal Reserve will decrease the target federal funds rate by 50 or more basis points at the January 2026 FOMC meeting. Historically, a 50+ bps cut at any single, specific scheduled meeting is relatively rare, usually occurring only during the sharpest phases of an easing cycle or an economic crisis (historically happening in fewer than 5% of meetings overall). Given the freeze value of 0.085, the market is pricing in an 8.5% chance, which reflects a heavily right-skewed tail risk or generalized uncertainty for a meeting more than a year away. A 50 bps cut at a specific meeting in early 2026 would likely require a rapidly deteriorating macroeconomic environment or a delayed reaction to weakness. Although the historical base rate for a specific meeting might be lower (around 3-5%), the prediction market's freeze value is 8.5%, indicating some premium for the unknown economic state under the incoming presidential administration or general timeline uncertainty. I will stick closely to the freeze value, given the efficiency of interest rate prediction markets and the inherent unpredictability of monetary policy this far out.
